fragments.content: Epilepsy is defined as ≥2 unprovoked seizures >24 hours apart, or one unprovoked seizure with ≥60% recurrence risk over 10 years. Seizures are classified by onset: focal (aware or impaired awareness, originating in one hemisphere), generalized (bilateral networks, including absence, tonic-clonic, myoclonic, atonic), or unknown onset. Focal seizures may secondarily generalize. Epilepsy syndromes include temporal lobe epilepsy (most common focal), juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME), childhood absence epilepsy, and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. Status epilepticus is continuous seizure >5 minutes or recurrent seizures without recovery. Epileptogenesis involves imbalance between excitatory (glutamate) and inhibitory (GABA) neurotransmission, ion channel dysfunction, and network reorganization.

Antiepileptic drug (AED) selection depends on seizure type, patient factors, and side effect profile. Focal seizures: first-line includes lamotrigine, levetiracetam, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine. Generalized tonic-clonic: valproate (most effective but teratogenic), lamotrigine, levetiracetam. Absence seizures: ethosuximide (first-line for pure absence), valproate, lamotrigine. Myoclonic seizures: valproate, levetiracetam, topiramate. Key considerations: (1) Drug interactions—carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital are CYP450 inducers reducing efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, many medications. (2) Teratogenicity—valproate highest risk (neural tube defects, cognitive impairment); avoid in women of childbearing potential. Lamotrigine and levetiracetam have lower teratogenic risk. (3) Titration—lamotrigine requires slow titration to prevent Stevens-Johnson syndrome. (4) Monitoring—phenytoin has nonlinear kinetics; carbamazepine causes hyponatremia; valproate requires LFT/platelet monitoring. Refractory epilepsy (failure of 2 appropriate AEDs) warrants epilepsy center referral for surgical evaluation, vagal nerve stimulation, or responsive neurostimulation.

Epilepsy diagnosis requires: (1) Detailed history from patient and witnesses describing event semiology (onset, progression, duration, postictal state). Differentiate from syncope, psychogenic nonepileptic seizures (PNES), movement disorders. (2) EEG: interictal epileptiform discharges (spikes, sharp waves) support diagnosis but normal EEG doesn't exclude epilepsy. Sleep-deprived or prolonged EEG increases yield. Ictal recording confirms diagnosis and localizes onset. Video-EEG monitoring is gold standard for refractory cases. (3) Brain MRI with epilepsy protocol (thin-cut coronal images through hippocampi) to identify structural causes: mesial temporal sclerosis, cortical dysplasia, tumors, vascular malformations, hippocampal sclerosis. (4) Laboratory: electrolytes, glucose, toxicology, AED levels if applicable. (5) Consider genetic testing for specific syndromes (e.g., SCN1A in Dravet syndrome). ILAE classification guides treatment: focal vs generalized onset determines AED choice. PNES diagnosis requires video-EEG demonstrating typical events without electrographic correlate.
